results_PLS.title_prompt:
Answer this question from the uploaded files  
provide a simple title of the document so that it can be easily understood ${age}. Use plain everyday language. Avoid randomization, blinding, and phase details. Replace the term patients/subjects with people or participants. Use the term effects for effectiveness.  
Determine the age of participants from the source text. Use 'adolescents' if participants are between 12 to 17 years old. Use 'children' if participants are under 12 years old.  If the study includes both adults and younger participants, use 'adults and adolescents' or 'adults and children' accordingly. Use 'people' if participants are 18 years or older. 
Include “safety” in the title only if safety is the only primary endpoint. If both safety and efficacy are primary endpoints, mention only “effects” in the title. 
Mention the drug code (e.g. ABC123) to refer to the investigational product throughout the PLTS for trials. The only exception is a phase 4 trial of an approved drug: use the generic name (e.g. sacubitril) if the official study title and arms descriptions use the generic name in the clinical study report (CSR) and the protocol. Identify the phase of the trial from the title page of the clinical study report (CSR) and approval status from the Introduction section of the clinical study report (CSR).  
Get the disease name that was tested in the clinical study in the given document by given drug. The title should be in the format that starts with "A clinical trial to learn/find/compare/understand about/learn more about…” Full title of the clinical study report (CSR)) and the main objectives of the trial are the source for the plain language title. Use the title template structure to construct the full title.  
Structure of title: A clinical trial to learn more about the effects of "Drug code" when given with.... in people with "health condition" 
Reply in max 25 words.
Example outputs:  
Example 1: A clinical trial to learn more about the effects of AIN457 given as an infusion into a vein in people with axial spondyloarthritis 
Example 2: A clinical trial to learn more about the long-term safety of LCZ696 in children with heart failure 
Example 3: A clinical trial to learn about the effects of HDM201 in combination with azacitidine and venetoclax in people with acute myeloid leukemia 
Example 4: A clinical trial to learn more about the effects of QGE031 in adolescents and adults with peanut allergy 
Always give output like this example 
Give output in JSON in the key title. 
{ 
  "title": "<output here>" 
}

results_PLS.health_condition_description_prompt:
Answer this question from the uploaded files  
readability level of you answer should be ${age}
Write about the health condition from the introduction section of the source document provided. Explain the health condition using plain everyday language in simple language in short sentences up to 15 to 25 words per sentence. Include the plain description of the disease, common symptoms and its impact. Be concise and use plain language suitable for someone at "${age}.  

Word limit for this Answer is 90 words. 

Examples:  

Example 1. Plaque psoriasis is a long-term skin condition that causes red, bumpy, and scaly patches called plaques on the skin. Psoriasis can appear anywhere on the body but mainly affects the elbows, knees, and scalp. Typical symptoms of plaque psoriasis include dry skin, rashes, itching, and burning sensation around the affected area.  

Example 2. Peanut allergy is a type of food allergy. In people with food allergies, the immune system considers certain food items or their content harmful and produces antibodies against them that cause an allergic reaction. Such food items or their content are called food allergens.  

Typical peanut allergy symptoms include skin rashes, itching or pain in the throat, diarrhea, vomiting, and shortness of breath. Sometimes people also experience life-threatening/severe symptoms such as a sudden drop in blood pressure, difficulty breathing, and loss of consciousness (this is known as anaphylaxis).  

Example 3: Acute myeloid leukemia (AML) is a blood cancer that starts in myeloid cells that are present in the bone marrow. Bone marrow is the tissue inside bones that helps make blood cells. Usually, myeloid cells develop into mature (fully developed and normal) blood cells such as red blood cells, white blood cells, and platelets (cells that help blood clot). In people with AML, these myeloid cells develop into abnormal blood cells (not fully developed, also called immature) that do not function properly. Common symptoms of AML are tiredness, weakness, frequent infections, fever, easy bruising, and bleeding.  

### FINAL JSON OUTPUT:
{
  "Health condition description": "<output here>"
}
results_PLS.drug_description_prompt:
<task_context>
Extract drug details for "[Drug Code]" and "[Generic Name]" from the protocol.
</task_context>

<naming_logic>
**CRITICAL:** Determine if a valid Generic Name exists.
- **Case A (Generic Name Exists):** If the document lists a generic name (e.g., "ligelizumab") that is different from the code.
  - *Format:* "[Drug Code], also called [Generic Name]..."
- **Case B (No Generic Name):** If the generic name is "N/A", "Not Available", or identical to the Drug Code (e.g., in early phase trials).
  - *Format:* "[Drug Code] is..." (Do NOT write "also called [Drug Code]").
</naming_logic>

<template_selector>
Based on the Naming Logic above + Approval Status, choose ONE strictly:

**Option 1: Investigational + Generic Name Exists**
"[Drug Code], also called [Generic Name], is a trial drug. It works by [15-20 word simplified mechanism]."

**Option 2: Investigational + NO Generic Name**
"[Drug Code] is a trial drug that works by [15-20 word simplified mechanism]."

**Option 3: Approved (Phase 4)**
"[Drug Code], also called [Generic Name], is approved for treating [indication]. It works by [15-20 word simplified mechanism]."
</template_selector>

<critical_constraints>
1. **Detail Ban:** Do NOT mention dosage frequency (e.g., "twice a day"), cycles, or combination therapies unless explicitly asked.
2. **Wordiness Ban:** Do NOT use phrases like "experimental cancer medicine" or "control how they grow." Use the exact templates above.
3. **Mechanism Simplification:** Keep mechanisms under 20 to 30 words words (e.g., "blocking a protein that helps cancer cells grow").
</critical_constraints>

<final_json_output>
Output ONLY valid JSON.
{
  "Drug description": "[Your strict template sentence here]"
}
</final_json_output>

results_PLS.treatment_prompt:
<task_spec>
Summarize the treatment period of the clinical trial, specifically focusing on the process, study design parts, and participant distribution.
</task_spec>

<readability_constraint>
Target Audience Age/Level: ${age}
- Adjust vocabulary and sentence complexity to match this level.
- Ensure the tone is informative but neutral.
</readability_constraint>

<content_requirements>
1. **Study Parts (Conditional)**: IF the 'Study Design' text explicitly mentions distinct parts (e.g., Part A, Part B), provide 1 sentence explaining them. IF NOT, ignore this step.
2. **Process Summary**: In 1-2 simple sentences, explain the intervention (e.g., "Participants took the drug/placebo for X weeks and visited the clinic for checks").
3. **Participant Flow**: Locate the 'Participant Flow' or 'Disposition' data.
   - Extract the total participant count.
   - Extract EVERY treatment arm/group with its exact name (including dose) and participant count.
</content_requirements>

<final_answer_formatting>
- Output MUST be valid JSON matching the schema below.
- "treatment_summary" combines the process summary and any study parts text.
- "groups" is an array of ALL treatment arms/groups (including placebo if applicable).
  Each group must have the exact name (with dose) and the number of participants.
- Do not include markdown formatting outside the JSON.
</final_answer_formatting>

<output_schema>
{
  "treatment_summary": "The process summary text here (1-2 sentences about the intervention).",
  "total_participants": 450,
  "groups": [
    {"name": "ABC123 120 mg", "participants": 55},

}

results_PLS.efficacy_primary_endpoint_results_conclu:
readability level of you answer should be ${age}

### TASK:

Report each primary endpoint results and the conclusion of every primary endpoint one after the other. Generate a comprehensive JSON object that fully summarizes the primary endpoint, its results, and data for the most appropriate chart. To do this, follow the sequence of instructions below precisely.

order of priority: Present the groups with study drug first and then the comparator or placebo.

there can be multiple endpoints for each endpoint you need to follow the below instruction.

### INSTRUCTIONS:

First, internally perform these analysis steps on the provided document:

1.  **Analyze the Primary Endpoint**:

    * Locate the "Primary Objective" section.

    * Rewrite the technical endpoint description as a simple, clear question suitable for a 6th grade readability level.

    * Analyze what the endpoint measures: Is it a comparison between groups at one time, a change over several time points, or parts of a whole?


2.  **Analyze the Primary Endpoint Results**:

    * Locate the "Primary Endpoint Results" section or table.

    * Extract the specific data points for each Dose level of each drug/placebo and/or time point.

    * Note the statistical conclusion (e.g., if the result was statistically significant).


3.  **Synthesize the Final Output**: Using the information you just gathered, construct the final JSON object with the following keys:

    * `question`: The simple question you created in Step 1.

    * `primary_endpoint_results_conclusion`, `clinical_term_definition`, `primary_endpoint_results_assessment`, `Primary_endpoint_results`: Plain-language text summaries as previously defined.

    * **`chart_type`**: Based on your analysis in Step 1, choose the single best chart type from this list:

        * `'bar_chart'`: **Use to compare** results between different, distinct groups (e.g., Placebo vs. Drug A vs. Drug B) at a single point in time.

        * `'line_chart'`: **Use to show a trend** of a single value changing over multiple, sequential time points (e.g., a score measured at Week 4, Week 8, and Week 12).

        * `'pie_chart'`: **Use to show proportions** of categories that make up a whole (e.g., percentage of participants with Mild, Moderate, or Severe reactions).

    * `chart_title`: Create a suitable title for the chart.

    * `data`: Create the JSON object for the chart. The structure of the labels and datasets should match the chosen chart type.

order in which the groups are presented in the chart is important. Present the groups with study drug first and then the comparator or placebo. example: ["XXB750 120/240 mg", "XXB750 120 mg", "XXB750 60 mg", "XXB750 30 mg", "Placebo"],

### FINAL JSON OUTPUT STRUCTURE:

Your final output must be a single JSON object matching this exact structure.

{
  "chart_data": [
    {
      "question": "<The simple question from your Step 1 analysis>",
      "primary_endpoint_results_conclusion": "<The one-sentence summary>",
      "clinical_term_definition": "<The definition of technical terms>",
      "primary_endpoint_results_assessment": "<The explanation of the assessment method>",
      "Primary_endpoint_results": "<The detailed sentence with numbers and percentages>",
      "chart_type": "<The chart type you chose: 'bar_chart', 'line_chart', or 'pie_chart'>",
      "chart_title": "<A suitable title for the chart>",
      "data": {
        "labels": ["<Labels appropriate for the chart type (e.g., Group Names or Time Points)>"],
        "datasets": [
          {
            "label": "<A suitable label for the data series>",
            "data": [<Value 1>, <Value 2>, <Value 3>]
          }
        ],
        "x_label": "<X-axis label, if applicable>",
        "y_label": "<Y-axis label, if applicable>"
      }
    }
  ]
}
results_PLS.key_secondary_endpoint_results_prompt:
### Persona & Core Directive
    readability level of you answer should be ${age}
Act as an expert AI system specializing in translating complex clinical trial documents into simple, understandable information. Your primary goal is to analyze the "Secondary Endpoints" section of a provided clinical study report (CSR). For each relevant secondary endpoint, you will generate a clear, plain-language question-and-answer pair and return all pairs in a single, valid JSON object.

### Guiding Principles
You must adhere to these principles at all times:

1.  **Absolute Accuracy:** All extracted data, results, and summaries must be factually correct and directly traceable to the source document.
2.  **Strict Source Adherence:** You must **never** infer, estimate, or fabricate information.
3.  **Clarity & Simplification:** All questions and answers must be written in plain, everyday English, appropriate for the person's age specified by `${age}`. Do not use technical jargon like "statistically significant."

### Step-by-Step Reasoning Process

**Step 1: Check Trial Status**
* First, determine if the trial was "completed as planned" or "terminated early."
* If the trial was **terminated early**, your entire output must be a JSON object with an empty list, like this: `{"Key secondary endpoint results": []}`. Stop processing further.

**Step 2: Identify & Filter Endpoints**
* If the trial was completed, proceed to the "Secondary Endpoints" (or equivalent) section of the CSR.
* Create a list of all secondary endpoints, but **exclude** any that are primarily about:
    * Safety
    * Pharmacokinetics (PK)
    * Pharmacodynamics (PD)
* From the filtered list, prioritize endpoints that are most relevant to a patient's direct experience and outcomes (e.g., symptom improvement, quality of life, functional ability).

**Step 3: Process Each Endpoint into a Q&A Pair (Loop)**
For each filtered, patient-relevant endpoint, create a complete question-and-answer pair by following these sub-steps:

* **A. Formulate the Question:**
    * Rewrite the endpoint as a simple question in the second person ("How much did...", "Was there a change...").
    * Include the specific parameter being measured (e.g., pain score, tumor size, quality of life).
    * Include the timeframe when the assessment was made. Convert time units for clarity (e.g., "Day 84" becomes "after 12 weeks"; "Day 365" becomes "after 1 year").

* **B. Find & Synthesize the Answer:**
    1.  Locate the results for that specific endpoint in the CSR.
    2.  Write the first part of the answer explaining how it was measured. (e.g., "To answer this question, doctors measured the change in tumor size using scans taken at the start of the study and after 6 months.")
    3.  Find the p-value associated with the result to determine how to present the findings:
        * **If p-value ≤ 0.05:** The result showed a difference. State the result clearly and qualitatively. (e.g., "In this study, people who received the new drug had a greater reduction in pain compared to those who received the placebo.")
        * **If p-value > 0.05:** The result did not show a clear difference. State this simply. (e.g., "In this study, researchers found the results for the two groups were similar." or "A clear difference between the groups could not be confirmed.")
    4.  Keep the entire answer to 2-3 concise sentences. Ensure the language makes it clear the results are only for "this study."

**Step 4: Assemble the Final JSON**
* Collect all the generated Q&A pairs.
* Structure them in the final JSON output as a list of objects.

### CRITICAL OUTPUT FORMAT
Your ENTIRE response MUST be a single, valid, parseable JSON object as described below. Do not include any introductory text or explanations outside of the JSON.

**JSON Structure:**
{
  "Key secondary endpoint results": [
    { "question": "<question 1 here,
         "answer": <answer 1 here>" },
    {  "question": "<question 2 here,
         "answer": <answer 2 here>"}
  ]
}

results_PLS.trial_adverse_event_overall_table_prompt:
If NovCTR results document is available, use that document for identifying this information. If not, look for this information in the clinical study report (CSR)
### TASK:
Generate a JSON object for the overall adverse event summary table. The structure must be followed exactly.
readability level of you answer should be ${age}
### INSTRUCTIONS:
1.  **Identify Groups**: From the safety analysis tables, identify the main treatment groups and their total participant counts (N) for the entire study period.
2.  **Create Headers**: Format the headers as a JSON list. The first item is " ", followed by "[Group Name]\n([N] participants)" for each Dose level of each drug/placebo.
3.  **Populate Rows**: For each event type below, find the count (n) for each group.
    * 'Had at least 1 serious adverse event'
    * 'Had at least 1 other adverse event'
    * 'Left the trial due to an adverse event'
    * 'Died'
4.  **Format Data**: Each row in the JSON must be a list of strings. The first string is the event name. Subsequent strings must be in the exact format: "n of N\npercentage%".
5.  **Assemble**: Combine into the final JSON object as shown in the example.
6. order in which the groups are presented in the chart is important. Present the groups with study drug first and then the comparator or placebo. example: ["XXB750 120/240 mg", "XXB750 120 mg", "XXB750 60 mg", "XXB750 30 mg", "Placebo"],
### EXAMPLE OF FINAL JSON OUTPUT:
{
  "trial_adverse_event_overall_table_prompt": {
    "headers": [
      " ",
      "Drug Arm A\n(99 participants)",
      "Drug Arm B\n(101 participants)"
    ],
    "rows": [
      ["Had at least 1 serious adverse event", "0 of 99\n0%", "1 of 101\n1%"],
      ["Had at least 1 other adverse event", "77 of 99\n78%", "74 of 101\n73%"],
      ["Left the trial due to an adverse event", "2 of 99\n2%", "0 of 101\n0%"],
      ["Died", "0 of 99\n0%", "0 of 101\n0%"]
    ]
  }
}

results_PLS.serious_adverse_event_table_prompt:
If NovCTR results document is available, use that document for identifying this information. If not, look for this information in the clinical study report (CSR)
### TASK:
Generate a JSON object for the table of the most frequent serious adverse events (SAEs). The structure must be followed exactly.
readability level of you answer should be ${age}
### INSTRUCTIONS:
To create the JSON, follow these steps in order:
1.  **Identify Treatment Groups and Headers**: From the safety analysis tables, identify the main treatment groups and their total participant counts (N). Create the `headers` list with the format `"[Group Name]\n([N] participants)"` for each Dose level of each drug/placebo.

2.  **Find the SAE Data**: Locate the table listing specific Serious Adverse Events in the 'Safety Results' section of the report.

3.  **Process Each Event**: For each SAE listed in that table that meets the reporting threshold:
    a.  Extract the technical **Preferred Term** (PT), for example, "Anaphylactic reaction".
    b.  Create a simple, **plain-language name** for the event, for example, "Severe allergic reaction".
    c.  For each treatment group, find the number of participants with the event (n) and the total participants in that group (N).
    d.  Format the data for each group into a single string with a newline: `"n of N\npercentage%"`. Remember to round percentages to a whole number.

4.  **Assemble the Rows**: Create the `rows` object, which must be a **list of lists**. Each inner list represents one row, where:
    * The first item is the row header, formatted exactly as: `"Plain-language name\nTechnical Term"`.
    * The following items are the formatted data strings for each treatment group.

5.  **Build the Final JSON**: Combine the `headers` and `rows` into the final JSON object as shown in the example below.
6. order in which the groups are presented in the chart is important. Present the groups with study drug first and then the comparator or placebo. example: ["XXB750 120/240 mg", "XXB750 120 mg", "XXB750 60 mg", "XXB750 30 mg", "Placebo"],
### CONCRETE EXAMPLE OF FINAL JSON OUTPUT:
{
  "serious_adverse_event_table_prompt": {
    "headers": [
      " ",
      "Drug 240mg\n(x participants)",
      "Placebo\n(y participants)"
    ],
    "rows": [
      [
        "Severe allergic reaction\nAnaphylactic reaction",
        "2 of x\n2%",
        "0 of y\n0%"
      ],
      [
        "Lung inflammation\nPneumonia",
        "1 of x\n1%",
        "1 of y\n1%"
      ]
    ]
  }
}

protocol_PLS.plain_language_title_prompt:
Answer this question from the uploaded  files.

Task: Rewrite the document title so it is clear and easy for a ${age}-year-old to understand.

Rules:
- Use plain everyday language.
- Avoid terms like “randomization,” “blinding,” and “phase.”
- Replace “patients” with “people with” or “participants in.”
- Begin with: "A clinical trial to learn more about the effects of..."
- Base the rewrite on the CSR full title and study objectives.
- Disease Condition: Extract the disease name tested in the study for the given drug.
- Drug Name: Use the generic drug name (e.g., "nirmatrelvir"), not the drug code. 
  - For nirmatrelvir/ritonavir, write as “nirmatrelvir and ritonavir combination.”
- Adjunctive Therapy: If mentioned, include as "(Drug name) when given with (Adjunctive therapy)."
- Structure: A clinical trial to learn more about the effects of "Drug name" when given with... in people with "Disease condition".
- Limit the title to a maximum of 25 words.
- If the disease name or other terms contain a hyphen, use Unicode ‑ instead (e.g., COVID‑19).
- Do not include any citations or references to source files.

Output:
Return only JSON in the format:
{"plain_language_title": "<rewritten title>"}

Examples:
1. {"plain_language_title": "A clinical trial to learn more about the effects of AIN457 given as an infusion into a vein in people with axial spondyloarthritis"}
2. {"plain_language_title": "A clinical trial to learn more about the effects of taking AIN457 while following a treatment strategy called treat-to-target in people with axial spondyloarthritis"}

protocol_PLS.Purpose_prompt:
Instructions:
Answer in future tense
• Read the uploaded source document and locate the “Primary Objectives section".
• Write the trial’s purpose in plain, everyday language at a ${age}. Avoid technical or scientific jargon.
• Keep a neutral, positive tone and be concise—focus on what the trial aims to achieve.  
Formatting Requirements:
• Output must be a JSON object with a single key, `"purpose statement"`.  
• Example based on Conditions:
If the primary objective is to study efficacy or both efficacy and safety of the drug, mention the below statement
 
  {
    "purpose statement": "The purpose of this trial is to learn about the effects of ABC123 in people with hypertension."
  }

  If the primary objective is to study the safety of the drug, mention the below statement
  {
    "purpose statement": "The purpose of this trial is to learn about the safety of ABC123 in people with hypertension."
  }  
• If the trial compares two treatments, state it like:
  {
    "purpose statement": "The purpose of this trial is to compare the effects/safety of Drug X with Comparator A in people with diabetes."
  }

• **Health condition**: Use the disease name as tested in the trial.  
• **Drug code**: Always use the investigational product code (e.g., ABC123). Do not use trade names, except in Phase 4 trials of approved drugs—then use the generic name if that’s how it appears in the clinical study report (CSR)/protocol.
• **Phase & Approval**: Identify the trial phase from the clinical study report (CSR) title page and the approval status from the Introduction; adjust terminology accordingly (e.g., use “sacubitril” only if it’s a Phase 4 generic in the protocol).

}
protocol_PLS.health_condition_description_prompt:
<task_context>
Analyze the "Introduction" section of the uploaded source document to identify the specific health condition being studied.
</task_context>

<verbatim_overrides>
CRITICAL INSTRUCTION: Before generating any text, check if the identified health condition matches exactly one of the examples below. If it matches, you MUST copy the text below verbatim and ignore the generation rules.

- **Plaque Psoriasis:** "Plaque psoriasis is a long-term skin condition that causes red, bumpy, and scaly patches called plaques on the skin. Psoriasis can appear anywhere on the body but mainly affects the elbows, knees, and scalp. Typical symptoms of plaque psoriasis include dry skin, rashes, itching, and burning sensation around the affected area."
- **Peanut Allergy:** "Peanut allergy is a type of food allergy. In people with food allergies, the immune system considers certain food items or their content harmful and produces antibodies against them that cause an allergic reaction. Such food items or their content are called food allergens. Typical peanut allergy symptoms include skin rashes, itching or pain in the throat, diarrhea, vomiting, and shortness of breath. Sometimes people also experience life-threatening/severe symptoms such as a sudden drop in blood pressure, difficulty breathing, and loss of consciousness (this is known as anaphylaxis)."
- **Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML):** "Acute myeloid leukemia (AML) is a blood cancer that starts in myeloid cells that are present in the bone marrow. Bone marrow is the tissue inside bones that helps make blood cells. Usually, myeloid cells develop into mature (fully developed and normal) blood cells such as red blood cells, white blood cells, and platelets (cells that help blood clot). In people with AML, these myeloid cells develop into abnormal blood cells (not fully developed, also called immature) that do not function properly. Common symptoms of AML are tiredness, weakness, frequent infections, fever, easy bruising, and bleeding."
</verbatim_overrides>

<generation_rules>
(Apply ONLY if the condition is NOT listed in the overrides above)
- **Content:** Explain the condition, including a plain description, common symptoms, and its impact.
- **Length:** Maximum 15 to 25 words.
- **Tone:** Objective, empathetic, and plain everyday language suitable for a ${age}-year-old.
- **Style:** Use active voice. Avoid medical jargon; if a term is necessary, define it immediately in parentheses.
</generation_rules>

<final_answer_formatting>
Return a valid JSON object. Do not output markdown code blocks or additional text.
{
  "Health condition description": "Your text here"
}
</final_answer_formatting>
protocol_PLS.drug_description_prompt:
<task_context>
Locate the investigational medicinal product details in the uploaded files.
**Search Strategy:** Look specifically in the 'Trial Design' and 'Trial Interventions' sections (Study Treatment, Dose, Mode of Administration, Mechanism of Action, Batch Numbers).
</task_context>

<extraction_requirements>
Identify the following elements before writing:
1. **Drug Code:** (e.g., ABC123)
2. **Generic Name:** (e.g., secukinumab). If none, Mention no Generic Name available.
3. **Mechanism of Action:** Simplify this to a physical analogy (blocking, binding, stopping).
4. **Approval Status:** (e.g., Trial Drug, approved drug for other uses).
</extraction_requirements>

<drafting_rules>
Construct a summary strictly following this logic:
- **Template:** "[Drug Code], also called [Generic Name], is [Description]."
- **Description Components:** Combine the *Mechanism of Action*, *Mode of Administration*, and *Approval Status* into the description.
- **Constraints:**
  - **Word Limit:** STRICTLY under 60 words.
  - **Tone:** Plain language, no jargon.
  - **Accuracy:** Do not oversimplify to the point of being incorrect.
</drafting_rules>

<examples>
Example 1: AIN457, also called secukinumab, works by blocking certain inflammation-causing proteins in the body, which can help reduce symptoms related to inflammation including itch, pain, and swelling.
Example 2: QGE031 is a type of antibody that binds to the antibody produced in response to the food allergen and prevents it from causing allergic reactions. In this trial, it is being studied for its effects on peanut allergy.
Example 3: HDM201, also called siremadlin, is a trial drug that aims to block a protein involved in the growth of cancer cells. By blocking this protein, researchers think it may treat certain types of cancers, including AML.
</examples>

<final_output_format>
Return ONLY a valid JSON object. Do not wrap it in markdown code markers (like ```json).
{
  "Drug description": "Insert your generated text here"
}
</final_output_format>

protocol_PLS.primary_endpoint_question_prompt:
<task_context>
Locate the "Primary Endpoint(s)" or "Primary Outcome Measure(s)" in the uploaded document. 
Note: There may be more than one. You must capture ALL primary endpoints.
</task_context>
this tense should be future tense. But since these are questions, "will" is not needed in this wording
<transformation_rules>
For EACH primary endpoint found, generate a response following these steps:
add bullet points before each question
1. **Draft the Question:** - Format: "What is the [parameter] at [time frame]?" or "How many participants [outcome] at [time frame]?"
   - **Constraint:** Maximum 25 words per question.
   - **Comparisons:** If the endpoint compares the drug to a placebo/comparator, append "compared to [comparator]" to the question.

2. **Draft the Explanation (Conditional):**
   - IF the specific endpoint parameter (e.g., "DAS28 score", "HbA1c") is technical or confusing, add a short explanation paragraph immediately after the question.
   - Start the explanation with "This measures..." or "This means..."
   - Keep it simple (Grade 6-8 reading level).

3. **Format & Terminology:**
   - **People:** Use "participants" (never "subjects" or "patients").
   - **Stats:** Use "percentage" (never "proportion").
   - **Time:** Convert Days $\to$ Weeks/Months (e.g., "Day 169" $\to$ "Week 24").
   - **Safety:** Replace any hyphens in disease names with the unicode non-breaking hyphen (\u2011) to prevent line breaks (e.g., "COVID-19" becomes "COVID\u201119").
</transformation_rules>

<examples>
Input: "Proportion of subjects with PASI 90 response at Day 112"
Output: "How many participants see a 90% improvement in their skin patches (psoriasis) at Week 16?\n\nThis measures how much the skin has cleared up."

Input: "Change from baseline in HbA1c at Week 24"
Output: "What is the change in blood sugar levels at Week 24 after taking the drug compared to placebo?\n\nHbA1c is a measure of your average blood sugar levels over the past 3 months."
</examples>

<final_answer_formatting>
- Combine all questions into a SINGLE string value.
- Separate distinct endpoints with double newlines (\n\n).
- Output valid JSON only.
</final_answer_formatting>

Output JSON:
{
  "primary endpoint question": " "
}
protocol_PLS.secondary_endpoint_question_prompt:
Task:
From the uploaded clinical trial document, identify all the secondary endpoints. Present each secondary endpoint as a question in a **clear, age-appropriate** way for ${age}.

Rules:
- Write a simple question that states the endpoint parameter + assessment time frame.
- if the endpoint parameter is not easy to understand, give a short plain language description right after the question.
- Ensure plain, everyday language that ${age} can understand.
- Replace any hyphen in disease/term names with \u2011 (e.g., "COVID-19" → "COVID\u201119").
- Use "\n" to mark new paragraphs.
- Do NOT include any source references or citations.
- Use "percentage" (not "proportion") and "participants" (not "subjects" or "patients").
- Convert days → weeks/months/years for clarity.
- if the endpoint was a comparative analysis, mention “after taking <drug code> compared to <comparator>” in the question.
- Output must be **only** the final JSON, no text before/after.
add bullet points before each question

Example secondary endpoint questions: 
•	How many participants show an improvement in their kidney function at Week 52 after receiving a single dose of YTB323?
•	How many participants have inactive SLE at Week 24 and Week 52?
Inactive SLE means that participants:
o	have no signs of active SLE
o	have a very low disease score, assessed by the trial doctor
o	need a daily dose of no more than 5 milligrams (mg) of steroids
o	did not take additional SLE medicines (rescue treatment) or strong medicines that stop the immune system from working
•	For how long does the participants’ SLE remain well controlled from Week 12 to 52?
•	How many participants do not have a severe SLE flare-up or more than 2 moderate SLE flare ups from Week 12 to 52 ?
SLE flare-ups are periods when the disease becomes active again. Moderate flare-ups cause symptoms like joint pain, rashes or tiredness whereas severe flare-ups are more serious, can affect vital organs, and need stronger treatment.
•	What is the average yearly dose of steroids that participants take up to Week 52?
•	How many participants test negative for antibodies specific to SLE at Week 52?
•	What is the change in the level of tiredness participants experience after 52 weeks of treatment?
•	How many participants have inactive SLE between Week 52 and Week 76?
•	How many participants regain color in at least 75% of the patches present on their faces at 24 weeks of treatment with different doses of GIA632 compared to placebo?
•	What is the improvement in patches present on a participant’s whole body after 24 weeks of treatment with different doses of GIA632 compared to placebo?
•	How many participants regain color in at least 50% of the patches present on their whole body at 24 weeks of treatment with different doses of GIA632 compared to placebo? 
•	How many participants regain color in at least 50%, 75%, and 90% of the patches present on their faces and whole body at 12, 24, 36, and 48 weeks of treatment with GIA632 compared to placebo?
•	How many participants have vitiligo that is much less visible or no longer visible at 12, 24, 36, and 48 weeks of treatment with GIA632 compared to placebo?
•	

Give output in JSON in the secondary endpoint question
{
  "secondary endpoint question": " "
}
